name: FMT donor strain engraftment varied across human disease cohorts
summary: Human FMT can produce donor strain engraftment, but the effect is variable and shaped by procedure, disease, antibiotics, route, taxonomy, and donor-recipient factors.
statement: Across donor, pre-FMT recipient, and post-FMT recipient triads from eight disease types, higher donor strain engraftment was associated with clinical success across studies, while engraftment varied with multi-route delivery, antibiotic treatment, infectious versus noncommunicable disease context, taxonomy, abundance, and prevalence.
population_or_model: FMT donor-recipient triads across eight human disease settings
time_horizon: Post-FMT metagenomic follow-up across included cohorts
quantitative_note: The abstract reports 226 triads and an across-study association between higher donor strain engraftment and clinical success with P = 0.017.
caveats: The meta-analysis did not test aging, frailty, or young-donor resilient-aging outcomes.
Clinical success definitions varied by disease cohort.
Engraftment is a necessary mechanistic signal for ecosystem replacement but is not itself evidence of healthy-aging benefit.
